evidence: `GenerationParams` permits arbitrary keys via `[key: string]: unknown`, so invalid/typo param names are accepted at compile time instead of producing a consistent failure signal.
No constrained error channel exists in this seam (e.g., discriminated invalid-param result or validated param shape), which makes boundary failures dependent on downstream consumers.
Boundary type mixes naming styles (`prompt` vs `base_prompt` / `negative_prompt` / `guidance_scale` / `num_inference_steps`) in a single interface, increasing seam translation ambiguity.
`index.ts` re-exports this unconstrained boundary type broadly, so the mixed contract propagates as a shared handoff surface.

findings.summary: Open index signature allows invalid params to pass without consistent failure behavior
evidence: `GenerationParams` includes `[key: string]: unknown`, so misspelled or unsupported keys are type-accepted.
The barrel export in `index.ts` makes this permissive contract a shared module boundary.
findings.suggestion: Replace the open index signature with explicit known fields plus a typed extension mechanism (e.g., tool-specific unions) and enforce validation at ingress so unsupported keys produce a consistent error path.

findings.summary: Generation param seam mixes camelCase and snake_case naming without a clear translation boundary
evidence: Interface includes both `prompt` and snake_case fields like `base_prompt`, `negative_prompt`, and `num_inference_steps` in the same handoff contract.
`index.ts` re-exports this type directly, spreading the mixed convention across consumers.
findings.suggestion: Establish one canonical app-facing convention (typically camelCase) and isolate snake_case mapping to a single persistence/API boundary mapper.
